indexed_incidents.title: Basement wall water ingress observed at construction joint
visual_clues: Dark damp streaks on cast wall surface
indexed_incidents.root_cause_hypothesis: Likely discontinuity in waterproofing membrane, inadequate joint preparation, or failed waterstop/sealant at a construction joint combined with hydrostatic pressure.
indexed_incidents.recommended_action: Mark and photograph the full extent of seepage and note elevation/chainage references.; Inspect the joint detailing, membrane continuity, and any penetrations or tie-hole repairs.; Check adjacent drainage, sump operation, and external water management conditions.; Implement temporary water control measures and isolate any affected electrical or finish-sensitive areas.; Raise a corrective action for waterproofing specialist review and retesting after repair.
indexed_incidents.escalation: Escalate immediately if leakage increases, reaches active electrical equipment, or shows signs of wall movement, cracking, or soil washout.

indexed_incidents.title: Concrete column honeycombing detected after form removal
indexed_incidents.root_cause_hypothesis: Probable inadequate vibration, congested reinforcement impeding flow, grout loss through formwork joints, or improper concrete placement technique.
indexed_incidents.recommended_action: Sound the affected area and carry out a depth assessment by engineer or competent inspector.; Verify reinforcement cover and determine whether structural repair is required.; Review pour sequence, vibration records, and formwork tightness for the affected lift.; Protect the area from impact and restrict loading until disposition is confirmed.; Prepare a repair method statement if patching, epoxy injection, or breakout and reinstatement is needed.
indexed_incidents.escalation: Escalate to structural engineer immediately if defect depth approaches reinforcement, cover is compromised, or multiple columns exhibit similar honeycombing.

indexed_incidents.title: Rebar congestion visible at beam-column joint
visual_clues: Reduced clear space for concrete flow
Difficulty aligning cover blocks or spacers
indexed_incidents.root_cause_hypothesis: Likely a combination of design-dense reinforcement at a highly loaded joint and insufficient constructability coordination between structural detailing and placement method.
indexed_incidents.recommended_action: Compare installed reinforcement against approved shop drawings and bar bending schedules.; Confirm minimum clear spacing, lap locations, and cover compliance before pour.; Coordinate with structural designer on any permissible bar adjustments or coupler alternatives.; Plan concrete placement with smaller aggregate, controlled lift heights, and dedicated vibration access.; Document the joint with pre-pour photos and sign-off before closing forms.
indexed_incidents.escalation: Escalate if required clear spacing cannot be achieved, if cover blocks cannot be installed correctly, or if the pour is proceeding without engineer approval.

indexed_incidents.title: MEP duct coordination clash at ceiling void
visual_clues: Hangers and supports appear tightly packed
Potential conflict with lighting or access panels
indexed_incidents.root_cause_hypothesis: Likely incomplete coordination of MEP trades, late design revisions, or insufficient clash detection before installation.
indexed_incidents.recommended_action: Verify current coordinated shop drawing against installed service positions.; Identify the conflicting elements and quantify the clearance shortfall.; Hold the affected trade install and route the clash to coordination review.; Confirm access requirements for dampers, valves, and future maintenance before finalizing layout.; Issue revised setting-out instructions and record any approved deviations.
indexed_incidents.escalation: Escalate to project coordination lead if ceiling closure is imminent, access to critical maintenance components is blocked, or multiple trade clashes are present.

indexed_incidents.title: Unsafe open edge adjacent to scaffold access point
visual_clues: Open edge without top rail or mid-rail
Scaffold access ladder or landing nearby
No visible toe board at edge
indexed_incidents.root_cause_hypothesis: Likely removal or omission of edge protection during sequencing, inadequate supervision, or incomplete scaffold-to-structure interface controls.
indexed_incidents.recommended_action: Stop access to the affected route until edge protection is installed or alternative safe access is provided.; Install compliant guardrails, toe boards, and controlled access measures at the perimeter.; Inspect scaffold tie-ins, landing condition, and opening protections for related defects.; Brief all nearby crews on the exclusion and safe access route.; Record the hazard in the permit or daily safety log and verify closure before restart.
indexed_incidents.escalation: Escalate immediately to site management and safety leadership; work in the area must remain stopped until the fall hazard is fully controlled.

indexed_incidents.title: Hairline crack observed near lift core wall opening
visual_clues: Localized around opening or stress concentration
No active leakage or spalling apparent
indexed_incidents.root_cause_hypothesis: Possible shrinkage crack, restraint from adjacent pours, temporary load transfer effects, or stress concentration at an opening corner.
indexed_incidents.recommended_action: Measure and mark crack length, width, and orientation using a calibrated gauge.; Check whether the crack is live by monitoring for seasonal or load-related movement.; Review recent pours, stripping sequence, and temporary loading around the core wall.; Inspect for associated moisture ingress, spalling, or adjacent cracking patterns.; Escalate for structural review if the crack widens, propagates, or appears through-thickness.
indexed_incidents.escalation: Escalate to structural engineer if crack width exceeds project tolerance, shows displacement, or is accompanied by leakage or progressive propagation.

phi4_role: CLIP image/text vectors retrieve the closest local cases from SQLite.
Phi-4-mini receives only the compact evidence list, so it can draft guidance without storing the case library in context.
The answer must cite incident IDs and preserve escalation rules from retrieved evidence.

answer_examples.query: A photo shows damp staining and active seepage along a basement retaining wall cold joint.
answer_examples.phi4_evidence_prompt:
Retrieved evidence:
[INC-001] score=0.691; title=Basement wall water ingress observed at construction joint; severity=high; image=notebooks/assets/cv_rag_enriched/images/inc_001_basement_wall_water_ingress_observed_at_cons.png; observation=Active water ingress is visible at the basement wall joint, with staining extending below the suspect construction joint and moisture tracking toward the floor slab edge. No immediate structural distress is evident, but the leakage suggests a compromised waterproofing detail or joint seal failure.; action=Mark and photograph the full extent of seepage and note elevation/chainage references.; Inspect the joint detailing, membrane continuity, and any penetrations or tie-hole repairs.; Check adjacent drainage, sump operation, and external water management conditions.; Implement temporary water control measures and isolate any affected electrical or finish-sensitive areas.; Raise a corrective action for waterproofing specialist review and retesting after repair.; escalation=Escalate immediately if leakage increases, reaches active electrical equipment, or shows signs of wall movement, cracking, or soil washout.
[INC-006] score=0.596; title=Hairline crack observed near lift core wall opening; severity=medium; image=notebooks/assets/cv_rag_enriched/images/inc_006_hairline_crack_observed_near_lift_core_wall_.png; observation=A narrow crack is visible near the lift core wall, close to an opening or re-entrant corner. At the moment it appears to be a hairline surface condition, but its location warrants monitoring because core walls can attract stress concentrations and construction-stage movement.; action=Measure and mark crack length, width, and orientation using a calibrated gauge.; Check whether the crack is live by monitoring for seasonal or load-related movement.; Review recent pours, stripping sequence, and temporary loading around the core wall.; Inspect for associated moisture ingress, spalling, or adjacent cracking patterns.; Escalate for structural review if the crack widens, propagates, or appears through-thickness.; escalation=Escalate to structural engineer if crack width exceeds project tolerance, shows displacement, or is accompanied by leakage or progressive propagation.
[INC-002] score=0.548; title=Concrete column honeycombing detected after form removal; severity=high; image=notebooks/assets/cv_rag_enriched/images/inc_002_concrete_column_honeycombing_detected_after_.png; observation=A reinforced concrete column shows honeycombing in the lower lift area following formwork removal. The defect is localized but extends over enough surface area to indicate poor consolidation or leakage during casting, requiring assessment of depth and impact on cover.; action=Sound the affected area and carry out a depth assessment by engineer or competent inspector.; Verify reinforcement cover and determine whether structural repair is required.; Review pour sequence, vibration records, and formwork tightness for the affected lift.; Protect the area from impact and restrict loading until disposition is confirmed.; Prepare a repair method statement if patching, epoxy injection, or breakout and reinstatement is needed.; escalation=Escalate to structural engineer immediately if defect depth approaches reinforcement, cover is compromised, or multiple columns exhibit similar honeycombing.

Offline CV-RAG answer from local image vectors and incident records.
Question: A photo shows damp staining and active seepage along a basement retaining wall cold joint.

Write a concise construction incident response. Include top visual match, action steps, escalation condition, and citations.

Phi-4-mini role in production:
1. Read the compact evidence list returned by local vector search.
2. Turn the top visual matches into a field-ready checklist with citations.
3. Keep the answer grounded; do not invent policy or override escalation rules.

Recommended response:
1. Compare the current site photo with the cited incident image and observation.
2. Apply only the corrective action that matches the field condition.
3. Escalate critical safety or structural issues before work continues.

Offline limitation: no cloud search, no remote standards lookup, and no supervisor approval automation.
